metadata.title: The Creative Brief: 6 Fields That Drive Everything
metadata.playlist: Make It Visible — Visual Identity & Wireframes
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 (Visual) brief — creative brief from A6, visual identity components, wireframes; due 2026-07-17

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 1 of 6. You have a product from A6. You know what it does and who it is for. This series gives it a face. A visual identity is what makes a user recognize the product on a crowded screen and feel like it belongs to a category they already trust. This episode is the creative brief — the six-field document you write before opening any design tool. If you skip this step and go straight to logos and colors, you will iterate on aesthetics without a way to know if you are getting closer or just changing things.

props.segment: The Creative Brief: 6 Fields That Drive Everything
props.command: I have my A6 product. Build a creative brief that drives every visual decision from logo to wireframes — 6 fields, nothing skipped.
props.runningText: six fields before any design tool opens — brand name, description, audience, adjectives, color direction, inspiration brands…
output: Brand name (2–3 syllables, favicon test) · One-sentence description from A6
Target audience (situation, not demographic) · 3 adjectives pointing the same direction
Color direction (domain-based, not hex) · Inspiration brands with one-sentence 'because' each

beats.narration_text: The creative brief has six fields. Field one: brand name. The short version a user would say out loud — two or three syllables if possible, because the logo will need to fit in a 32-pixel favicon. Field two: one-sentence description. What the product does and who it is for, in one sentence. Pull this directly from your A6 product statement. Field three: target audience. Not a demographic — a situation. Who the primary user is, what they are trying to do, what they do not have time for. Field four: tone and personality. Three adjectives. Precise ones — not 'professional' but 'calm and precise' or 'energetic and approachable.' The adjectives are the brief a logo designer acts on. Field five: color direction. Two or three colors from the product's domain, not hex codes yet — a direction. Field six: inspiration brands. Two or three brands with the visual energy you want, each with a one-sentence because. The because is more important than the brand name — it tells the tool which specific quality you are borrowing.

props.artifactHeading: Write this before opening any design tool
artifactLines: 1. Brand name — 2–3 syllables, passes the 32px favicon test.
2. One-sentence description — what it does + who it's for (from A6).
3. Target audience — a situation, not a demographic.
4. Tone + personality — 3 adjectives that all point the same direction.
5. Color direction — domain-based observation, not hex codes yet.
6. Inspiration brands — 2–3, each with a one-sentence 'because'.
props.sparkLine: The brief is done when someone who's never seen your A6 product can make a design decision you'd approve.

beats.act: fields 1 and 2 — name and description from A6
beats.narration_text: Fields one and two come directly from A6. If your product statement says 'an AI-powered meal-planning tool for college students who want to eat well on a budget,' the brand sentence is that sentence — copy it. The brand name is whatever you have been calling the product since A6. If that name is a full phrase, shorten it now. Two to three syllables is the test. The favicon test is real: write the short name at 32 pixels in your head. If it fits, the name works. If it does not, pick a different name and note the reasoning in the brief — not because the grader requires it, but because every downstream AI tool will ask you why the name changed from A6. Your answer should be in the brief so you do not have to reconstruct it later.

props.artifactHeading: Both come directly from A6 — copy, then compress
artifactLines: Brand name: the short version (2–3 syllables). Favicon test: write it at 32px in your head.
If the A6 name is a full phrase, shorten it now and note the reason in the brief.
One-sentence description: copy the A6 product statement exactly.
Every downstream tool will ask why the name changed from A6. Document it here.
These two fields are the anchor — every other field is built to match them.
props.sparkLine: Copy the A6 product statement. Compress the name. Document any change.

beats.narration_text: Target audience is the most underspecified field in most student briefs. Do not write 'college students aged 18 to 22.' Write the user's situation: 'first-generation college students who track their dining-hall budget manually and want a faster way to plan meals without giving up home-food preferences.' That sentence tells a designer something a demographic does not. A designer reading that sentence knows the UI cannot be intimidating, the product should feel like a recommendation from a smart friend, and the product photograph should not look like a meal-kit ad. Three adjectives in field four should all point in the same direction. If you write 'bold, minimal, friendly,' one of those three is doing different work than the others. Pick the three that agree. The test: if you finished the visual identity and asked a stranger to describe it in three words, would those words match your adjectives? That is the brief working.

props.artifactHeading: A situation, not a demographic. Three adjectives that agree.
artifactLines: Field 3 — target audience: write the user's situation, not a demographic range.
Example: 'students who track their budget manually and want a faster way without losing home-food preferences.'
A situation tells a designer: UI tone, photography style, information density.
Field 4 — 3 adjectives: 'bold, minimal, friendly' don't agree. Pick three that point the same direction.
Test: would a stranger describe your finished logo with these exact three words?
props.sparkLine: Situation beats demographic. Adjectives that agree beat adjectives that compromise.

beats.act: fields 5 and 6 — color direction and inspiration brands
beats.narration_text: Color direction in field five is not a hex code — it is a domain observation. A fintech tool for cautious first-time investors: 'institutional blues, no reds, one green for positive returns.' An art-community platform: 'high saturation, editorial palette, primary colors used with confidence.' A health product: 'clean whites, one green, nothing that reads as pharmaceutical.' The direction comes from the product's domain and the audience's expectations about what that domain looks like. Inspiration brands in field six work best when the because is specific. Not 'Airbnb because it looks clean' — instead 'Airbnb because it uses photography as the primary hero element and keeps UI chrome minimal so the content leads.' That level of specificity is what tells nina which part of the brand you are borrowing.

props.artifactHeading: Domain observation, not hex codes. Specific becauses, not vibe.
artifactLines: Field 5 — color direction: observe the domain. Fintech → 'institutional blues, no reds, one green.'
Health → 'clean whites, one green, nothing that reads as pharmaceutical.'
The direction comes from what users expect the domain to look like.
Field 6 — inspiration brands: the 'because' is more important than the brand name.
Not 'Airbnb because clean' — 'Airbnb because photography is the primary hero, chrome is minimal.'
props.sparkLine: Specific becauses tell nina which quality you're borrowing, not the whole brand.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Six fields: brand name, one-sentence description, target audience, three adjectives, color direction, two to three inspiration brands with becauses. The brief is done when someone who has never seen your A6 product could read it and make a design decision you would approve. Test it now: read your brief and ask whether you could tell immediately if a proposed logo is on-brief or off-brief. If yes, the brief is working. If no, one field is underspecified — the adjectives or the color direction are the most common places. Write the brief before opening any design tool, including nina. The brief is the instructions; every tool you use after this point is executing those instructions.

props.artifactTitle: The Creative Brief: 6 Fields That Drive Everything
props.artifactHeading: Brief done = you can immediately tell on-brief from off-brief
artifactLines: 6 fields: name · description · audience · adjectives · color direction · inspiration brands.
Test: read the brief. Can you immediately judge whether a proposed logo is on-brief?
If no → one field is underspecified. Adjectives and color direction fail most often.
Write the brief before opening nina, Figma, Coolors, or any other tool.
Every downstream decision — logo, palette, fonts, wireframes — executes this brief.
The brief is the instructions. Tools execute. Human judges.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the nina prompt for the creative brief. Paste it with your A6 product information already in context. The prompt reads: Here is my A6 product — name, one-sentence description, target audience, three adjectives, color direction, two to three inspiration brands with becauses. Using these six fields, generate a complete creative brief for a visual identity. Format: one paragraph per field, labeled. Include a one-sentence brief summary at the top that a designer could act on immediately. Flag any field where my inputs are underspecified and suggest a more precise version. After running the prompt: read the brief summary at the top. If you would not hire a designer based on that one sentence, the inputs are not specific enough. Rewrite the underspecified fields before moving to the next episode.

props.segment: The Creative Brief: 6 Fields That Drive Everything
props.command: Here is my A6 product — name: [name], description: [one sentence], audience: [situation], adjectives: [3], color direction: [domain observation], inspiration brands: [2–3 with becauses]. Generate a complete creative brief. Format: one paragraph per field, labeled. Include a one-sentence brief summary at the top a designer could act on. Flag any underspecified field and suggest a more precise version.
props.runningText: read the brief summary at the top — if you wouldn't hire a designer based on that one sentence, the inputs need work before Episode 2…

beats.narration_text: The Creative Brief: 6 Fields That Drive Everything. Liam, in for Bear.
